title: Technical Roadmap Planning
description: OKR writing for platforms (“Reduce batch P99 from 30s to 5s”), North Star metrics, horizon 1/2/3 planning, tech debt management (quantification, repayment scheduling), communicating strategy.

description.value:
Objectives and Key Results (OKRs) are how FAANG aligns execution with strategy. For SAP platform engineers, writing effective OKRs is challenging because platform work is invisible — you can't point to a feature shipped. Instead, you must measure platform health, developer velocity, and reliability. Staff+ engineers are expected to write OKRs that connect infrastructure investments to business outcomes.

A well-formed OKR has an Objective (qualitative, inspirational) and 3–5 Key Results (quantitative, measurable). Bad OKR: "Improve batch processing." Good OKR: "Objective: Make batch processing invisible to business users. KR1: Reduce P99 batch runtime from 30s to 5s. KR2: Reduce batch failure rate from 2% to 0.1%. KR3: Eliminate business-user-initiated batch resubmissions." The key results must be measurable without interpretation — if two people can look at a KR and disagree on whether it's met, it's not well-defined.

Platform OKR Anti-Patterns: The most common mistake is output-based KRs ("Ship new BAPI interface") instead of outcome-based KRs ("Reduce integration failure rate by 50% using new BAPI interface"). Another anti-pattern is non-measurable KRs ("Improve system quality") — always ask: "What's the unit of measurement? What's the baseline? What's the target?" A third anti-pattern is KRs that the team doesn't control ("Increase SAP license savings by 20%" — this depends on procurement decisions outside engineering scope).

For SAP platforms specifically, good Key Results include: Reliability (batch success rate, MTTR, availability %), Performance (P50/P99 latency, throughput per SAPS, query response time), Developer Velocity (time from commit to production, number of manual steps in deployment, test coverage), and Cost Efficiency (HANA memory utilization, AWS/Azure spend per workload, license utilization). Pick 2–3 dimensions per quarter — trying to move all levers simultaneously dilutes focus.

y1="100" x2="350" y2="120" class="okr-edge"/><rect class="okr-kr" x="40" y="120" width="620" height="32"/><text class="okr-sub" x="350" y="140">KR1: Reduce P99 batch runtime from 30s to 5s (measurement: Prometheus)</text><rect class="okr-kr" x="40" y="160" width="620" height="32"/><text class="okr-sub" x="350" y="180">KR2: Reduce batch failure rate from 2% to 0.1% (measurement: job monitoring)</text><rect class="okr-kr" x="40" y="200" width="620" height="32"/><text class="okr-sub" x="350" y="220">KR3: Eliminate business-user-initiated batch resubmissions (measurement: ticket count)</text><rect class="okr-box" x="40" y="250" width="290" height="80" stroke="var(--svg-error-stroke)"/><text class="okr-lbl" x="185" y="272">Bad KRs</text><text class="okr-sub" x="185" y="292">"Improve batch processing"</text><text class="okr-sub" x="185" y="306">"Ship new interface" (output, not outcome)</text><text class="okr-sub" x="185" y="320">"Increase license savings" (not controllable)</text><rect class="okr-box" x="370" y="250" width="290" height="80" stroke="var(--svg-success-stroke)"/><text class="okr-lbl" x="515" y="272">Good KRs</text><text class="okr-sub" x="515" y="292">P99 latency reduction (measurable)</text><text class="okr-sub" x="515" y="306">Failure rate reduction (measurable)</text><text class="okr-sub" x="515" y="320">Ticket volume reduction (measurable)</text></svg>

# OKR Template for SAP Platform Teams

# Objective (Qualitative, Inspirational)
Objective: Make batch processing invisible to business users

# Key Results (Quantitative, Measurable)
KR1: Reduce P99 batch runtime from 30s to 5s
  - Baseline: 30s (Q4 2025)
  - Target: 5s (Q2 2026)
  - Measurement: Prometheus batch_job_duration_seconds p99

KR2: Reduce batch failure rate from 2% to 0.1%
  - Baseline: 2% of batch jobs fail
  - Target: 0.1% failure rate
  - Measurement: batch_job_failure_count / batch_job_total_count

KR3: Eliminate business-user-initiated resubmissions
  - Baseline: 50 tickets/month for batch resubmission
  - Target: 0 tickets/month
  - Measurement: IT service desk ticket count

# Initiatives (What we will build)
I1: Implement automatic retry with exponential backoff for transient failures
I2: Add real-time batch progress dashboard in Fiori
I3: Migrate batch jobs from legacy SM37 to SAP BTP Job Scheduler

decisionTree.title: Writing Effective Platform OKRs
decisionTree.prompt: How do I write OKRs that capture platform value rather than just feature output?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Can this OKR be measured without interpretation?" If two people can look at the KR and disagree on whether it's met, it's not a valid KR. Rewrite until the measurement is unambiguous. OKRs should be outcome-based (what changed for the user) not output-based (what we shipped).

branches.condition: The KR measures something the team shipped or built (e.g., 'Ship new BAPI interface')
branches.path: This is an output-based KR — rewrite to outcome-based. Ask: what changes for the user? 'Reduce integration failure rate by 50% using new BAPI interface' is outcome-based. The output (ship BAPI) is an initiative under the KR, not the KR itself.

branches.condition: The KR uses vague language like 'improve' or 'enhance' without a baseline and target
branches.path: Add baseline and target with measurement source. 'Improve system quality' → 'Reduce P99 batch runtime from 30s to 5s (measurement: Prometheus batch_job_duration_seconds)'. Every KR must have: baseline value, target value, and measurement source.

branches.condition: The KR depends on factors outside the team's control (e.g., 'Increase SAP license savings by 20%')
branches.path: Rewrite to something the team directly controls. 'Reduce HANA memory consumption per workload by 20%' is controllable. 'Increase license savings' depends on procurement decisions — not a valid engineering KR.

branches.condition: The OKR connects a platform improvement to a business outcome and has measurable KRs
branches.path: Great! This is a well-formed OKR. Example: Objective: 'Make batch processing invisible to business users.' KR1: Reduce P99 batch runtime from 30s to 5s. KR2: Reduce failure rate from 2% to 0.1%. KR3: Eliminate business-user-initiated resubmissions.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The Staff+ skill is writing OKRs that connect platform health to business outcomes. For SAP platforms, use Reliability (MTTR, availability), Performance (P99 latency), Developer Velocity (time-to-prod), and Cost Efficiency (HANA memory utilization). Pick 2 per quarter max — more dilutes focus.

description.value:
A North Star Metric is the single metric that captures the core value your platform delivers to the business. It's the one number that, if you improve it, everything else falls into place. For Facebook, it's Daily Active Users. For Airbnb, it's Nights Booked. For an SAP platform team, your North Star might be Time-to-Close for Financial Periods — because every platform improvement (batch speed, data quality, integration reliability) ultimately serves the goal of closing books faster.

The North Star metric must be leading, not lagging. Lagging metrics (like "migration completed") are historical — you can't influence them today. Leading metrics (like "batch pipeline success rate") predict future outcomes. A good North Star is also comprehensible to non-engineers. When the CFO asks "What does the platform team do?", you answer: "We reduce financial close time from 8 days to 24 hours." That's a story everyone understands.

Counter-Metrics: Every North Star needs counter-metrics to prevent gaming. If your North Star is "reduce financial close time," teams might sacrifice accuracy for speed. The counter-metric is "post-close adjustment rate" — if close time drops but adjustments skyrocket, you're optimizing the wrong thing. Similarly, "deployment velocity" must be paired with "incident rate due to deployments." FAANG teams use dashboard pairs where North Star and counter-metric are displayed side by side on the same monitor.

Setting the North Star for SAP Platforms: Good candidates for an SAP platform North Star include: Developer Onboarding Time (hours from access request to first successful transport), Data Freshness (time from operational event to availability in reporting), Integration Success Rate (percentage of messages delivered successfully end-to-end), or System Availability during critical business windows (financial close, payroll run, month-end). Choose one that aligns with your organization's top priority for the fiscal year.

completed ✅</text><text class="ns-sub" x="515" y="204">Historical — can't influence today</text><rect class="ns-counter" x="40" y="225" width="290" height="36"/><text class="ns-sub" x="185" organizationalDimension.title: Adopting North Star Metrics Across an SAP Platform Organization
organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: Identify the single metric that captures the core value your SAP platform delivers — for most enterprises it's Financial Close Time or Time-to-Value for new integrations. Present the North Star concept to the VP of Engineering with a counter-metric to prevent gaming. Run a 3-month pilot where one squad tracks their North Star alongside existing KPIs. If the pilot shows alignment (North Star improvements correlate with business outcomes), roll out to all squads.

stakeholders.name: VP of Engineering
stakeholders.concern: One metric cannot capture all platform value. Teams will optimize for the metric at the expense of other dimensions.
stakeholders.engagement: Implement counter-metrics. Show correlation data. Review North Star + counter-metric dashboard quarterly.

stakeholders.name: Squad Leads
stakeholders.concern: North Star may conflict with squad-level OKRs that focus on feature delivery
stakeholders.engagement: Align North Star with squad OKRs. Each squad can have a squad-specific North Star that rolls up to the platform North Star.

stakeholders.name: Business Stakeholders (CFO, COO)
stakeholders.concern: Technical metrics (batch duration, latency) don't translate to business value
stakeholders.engagement: Translate North Star into business language: 'Reducing close time from 8 days to 24 hours saves $2M/year in finance labor and improves cash flow by $5M.'

activities: Interview business stakeholders to identify the #1 platform pain point
Analyze historical data to find metrics that correlate with business outcomes
Propose North Star metric with counter-metric
Weekly squad review of North Star dashboard
Quarterly correlation analysis with business KPIs
Template North Star and counter-metric for each platform domain
Build central North Star dashboard in Grafana/SAP Analytics Cloud
Include North Star in quarterly business reviews

metrics: North Star metric improvement (target: 30% improvement in 12 months)
Correlation coefficient between North Star and business outcome (target: > 0.7)
Number of squads with active North Star tracking (target: 100% after 2 quarters)
antiPatterns: North Star without counter-metric — teams will game the metric at the expense of quality
Too many North Stars — one per platform domain (batch, API, integration) but only one per team
Changing North Star every quarter — takes 6-12 months for a North Star to show trend

description.value:
Horizon planning is a framework popularized by McKinsey for managing innovation pipelines. It classifies initiatives into three horizons based on their time to impact: Horizon 1 (0–12 months) — core business optimization, Horizon 2 (12–24 months) — adjacent expansion, and Horizon 3 (24–48 months) — transformational bets. FAANG Staff+ engineers use this framework to balance short-term delivery with long-term platform evolution.

Horizon 1 — Optimize the Core: These are initiatives that improve existing systems with predictable ROI. For an SAP platform, H1 includes: migrate PI/PO interfaces to Event Mesh (faster, cheaper, more reliable), upgrade ECC to S/4HANA (maintenance compliance, performance gains), automate manual transport approvals (velocity gain), and add monitoring dashboards for existing batch jobs. These initiatives should have clear metrics, fixed timelines, and documented success criteria. H1 typically consumes 60–70% of engineering capacity.

Horizon 2 — Expand the Adjacent: These are initiatives that build new capabilities adjacent to existing ones. For SAP: build a self-service API gateway for business partners to access order data, implement a data mesh architecture for self-service analytics, create an internal developer platform (IDP) with golden paths for SAP extension development. H2 initiatives have higher uncertainty but higher upside. They consume 20–25% of capacity. Each H2 initiative should have a kill criterion — a pre-defined signal that tells us to stop investing if the hypothesis doesn't pan out.

Horizon 3 — Transformational Bets: These are high-risk, high-reward initiatives that could reshape the platform. For SAP: build an AI-powered ERP assistant using SAP AI Core + LLMs, replace the legacy ABAP stack with a cloud-native runtime, create an industry-specific ERP template for new markets. H3 initiatives consume 5–10% of capacity. They are funded as time-boxed explorations (3–6 months) with a clear "go/no-go" decision point. Most H3 bets will fail — and that's acceptable, as long as we learn fast.

Staff+ engineers must be able to present their roadmap as a horizon portfolio, not as a flat list of projects. When asked "What's on your roadmap?", organize your answer by horizon: "Here's what we're shipping this quarter (H1), here's what we're exploring for next year (H2), and here's the bet that could transform our architecture (H3)." This signals strategic thinking and the ability to manage the exploration-exploitation tradeoff.

Technical debt is the silent killer of SAP platforms. A single ECC system that has been customized for 20+ years accumulates thousands of Z-programs, custom BADIs, and modified standard objects. Each modification increases the cost of the next upgrade. FAANG Staff+ engineers are expected to quantify, prioritize, and systematically retire tech debt — not just complain about it.

Quantifying Tech Debt: You can't manage what you can't measure. Quantify tech debt along three axes: Maintenance Cost (hours spent per quarter fixing issues in this component), Upgrade Impact (how many objects must be re-mediated for the next S/4HANA upgrade), and Risk Score (probability of failure × business impact). A simple formula: Tech Debt Score = (Maintenance Hours × 1.0) + (Critical Objects × 2.5) + (Risk Score × 5.0). Components with the highest score get prioritized for remediation.

Debt Repayment Scheduling: The 2:1 Rule — for every new feature, spend twice as much time paying down debt in the same area. If you add a new BADI implementation, spend 2 hours refactoring related legacy code. This prevents debt from growing faster than repayment. For systemic debt (like 500 custom Z-programs blocking S/4 upgrade), create a dedicated debt repayment sprint every quarter with a fixed capacity allocation (e.g., 20% of team bandwidth).

Debt Categories: Prudent-Deliberate ("We knew the custom BADI was tech debt, but we needed to ship the feature — we documented it and scheduled remediation") — this is acceptable. Reckless-Deliberate ("We know we should write unit tests, but we skip them without tracking") — this is irresponsible. Prudent-Inadvertent ("We followed SAP best practices, but a year later the standard changed") — unavoidable. Reckless-Inadvertent ("We didn't know about the standard enhancement framework") — requires training.

For SAP-specific tech debt, focus on: Modified SAP Standard Objects (highest cost, must be eliminated for cloud readiness), Custom Code Without Unit Tests (no safety net for regression), Outdated BADIs (still using pre-enhanced BADIs instead of BAdI enhancements), Hardcoded Configuration (company code, plant IDs in code instead of BAdI tables), and Monolithic Report Programs (>1000-line reports that should be modular classes).

x="350" y="282">SAP Debt Quantification Formula</text><text class="td-sub" x="350" y="302">Score = (Maintenance Hrs × 1.0) + (Critical Objects × 2.5) + (Risk Score × 5.0)</text><text class="td-sub" x="350" y="320">Prioritize components with highest score for remediation</text><text class="td-sub" x="350" y="338">2:1 Rule — 2 hours debt repayment per 1 hour new feature</text></svg>

# Tech Debt Scoring & Prioritization

# Formula
Tech Debt Score = (Maintenance Hours * 1.0) + (Critical Objects * 2.5) + (Risk Score * 5.0)

# Example: Legacy BADI Implementation
# - Maintenance Hours: 40 hours/quarter spent on BADI issues
# - Critical Objects: 12 custom BADIs blocking S/4 upgrade
# - Risk Score: 8/10 (core financial process, high impact if fails)

Score = (40 * 1.0) + (12 * 2.5) + (8 * 5.0)
      = 40 + 30 + 40 = 110

# Priority Ranking
| Component | Score | Category | Action |
|-----------|-------|----------|--------|
| Legacy BADIs (FI) | 110 | Deliberate | Q1 remediation |
| Z-Programs (SD) | 85 | Reckless | Q2 refactor |
| Modified VBAP | 200 | Deliberate | Urgent — S/4 blocker |
| Hardcoded configs | 45 | Inadvertent | Q3 training + fix |

# Repayment Strategy
- Q1: 20% capacity debt sprint (VBAP, BADIs)
- Q2: 20% capacity debt sprint (Z-programs)
- Ongoing: 2:1 rule for all new features
- Tracking: Tech debt dashboard in Grafana

adversarialResponse.title: When Business Stakeholders Block Tech Debt Repayment
adversarialResponse.challenge: You've quantified tech debt in your SAP platform: 3,200 Z-programs, 400 modified standards, 150 custom BADIs. The S/4HANA upgrade is blocked until 800 critical programs are remediated. You propose dedicating 30% of team capacity to debt repayment for 3 quarters. The Product VP pushes back: 'Features are what the business pays for. Tech debt is your problem. I can't tell my stakeholders we're slowing down features so engineers can refactor code that already works.' The debt repayment sprint is deprioritized for the third consecutive quarter.
adversarialResponse.badResponse: Escalate to the CTO and demand the 30% allocation. The CTO sides with the Product VP ('features pay the bills'). The engineering team becomes demoralized — they see the debt growing and feel powerless. Attrition increases. The S/4 upgrade is delayed by 12 months, costing $2M in extended ECC maintenance. The CTO blames engineering for not planning better.
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: Change the framing from 'tech debt' to 'upgrade risk': (1) Quantify the dollar cost of delay: 'Every month we delay S/4 remediation costs $167K in extended ECC maintenance and $50K in incident-related downtime. Total: $217K/month.' (2) Reframe debt repayment as an investment with ROI: 'Investing $900K in debt repayment saves $2.6M/year in maintenance and avoids a $5M emergency migration.' (3) Propose a compromise: 20% capacity for debt plus a '1:1 offset' — for every feature story, the team also completes one debt story of equal size in the same area. (4) Get a joint sponsor: the VP of Engineering and the Product VP co-sponsor the initiative, sharing accountability for both feature velocity and platform health. Result: 20% allocation approved. Debt repayment starts in Q1. S/4 upgrade completes on schedule.
adversarialResponse.pattern: Reframing pattern: translate technical problems into business risks with dollar figures. Tech debt is not an engineering concern — it's a business risk with measurable financial impact. Use the 'cost of delay' framing popularized by Don Reinertsen to make the business case for platform investments.

description.value:
The best strategy is worthless if you can't communicate it. FAANG Staff+ engineers spend 30–50% of their time in communication activities: writing strategy docs, presenting at engineering all-hands, facilitating cross-team alignment meetings, and writing RFCs. For SAP architects, the communication challenge is compounded by the need to bridge business stakeholders (who speak "SAP") and engineering teams (who speak "distributed systems").

The Strategy Memo: FAANG executives expect decisions to be communicated as memos, not slide decks. A strategy memo is 1–2 pages, written in plain English, with a clear recommendation at the top. The structure: Context (what's changing in the business or technology landscape), Problem (why the current approach won't work), Proposal (what we should do, with timeline and resource estimate), Risks (what could go wrong and how we mitigate), and Decision Required (an explicit ask: "Approval to proceed with Phase 1 of Event Mesh migration, budget: $500K").

The 3-Box Strategy: A useful framework for communicating strategy to teams. Box 1: Where we are (current state with data — "Our batch failure rate is 2%, costing $1M/year in operational overhead"). Box 2: Where we want to be (target state with North Star metric — "Batch failure rate 0.1%, zero manual resubmissions"). Box 3: How we get there (roadmap with milestones — "Q1: Add auto-retry, Q2: Migrate top 20 jobs, Q3: Decommission legacy scheduler"). This framework works for executive presentations, team meetings, and one-on-ones.

Narrative & Storytelling: Data without narrative is noise. When presenting strategy, lead with a story. "Last month, a batch job failed at 2 AM. The on-call engineer spent 4 hours debugging, the financial close was delayed, and the CFO escalated to the CEO. We've had 12 such incidents this year. Here's how we prevent them." Stories create emotional engagement that raw data doesn't. FAANG interviewers specifically evaluate storytelling ability in behavioral rounds — they want to see that you can make technical strategy compelling to non-technical stakeholders.

Common communication anti-patterns for SAP architects: using too many acronyms (CO-PA, MM-SRV, SD-SHP), leading with technology instead of business outcomes ("We'll implement Event Mesh" vs "We'll reduce integration failures by 80%"), and defaulting to SAP-standard language instead of articulating tradeoffs. Practice translating every SAP initiative into business outcome language before presenting to leadership.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Strategy Memo vs Slide Deck for Communicating Platform Roadmap

variables.name: T_memo
variables.description: Time to write a 1-page strategy memo (hours)

variables.name: T_deck
variables.description: Time to prepare a 15-slide presentation deck (hours)

variables.name: R_memo
variables.description: Read rate of a strategy memo within 1 week (executives)

variables.name: D_memo
variables.description: Average days from memo submission to decision

variables.name: D_deck
variables.description: Average days from presentation to decision (scheduling delay)

variables.name: N_decisions
variables.description: Number of significant strategy decisions per quarter

variables.name: C_delay
variables.description: Cost per week of delayed decision (dollars)

calculation.formula: Memo cost = T_memo × blended_rate × N_decisions × 4. Deck cost = T_deck × blended_rate × N_decisions × 4. Delay cost_memo = D_memo/7 × C_delay × N_decisions × 4. Delay cost_deck = D_deck/7 × C_delay × N_decisions × 4.
calculation.ap: Slide deck: 20h × $125 × 8 × 4 = $80,000/year. Delay cost: 21/7 × $50,000 × 8 × 4 = $4,800,000/year. Total: $4,880,000. But retention is 40% vs 85% — hiddent cost of misunderstood decisions adds another $500K.
calculation.breakeven: Memo saves $3.7M/year in decision delay alone, plus $64K in preparation time. The 4:1 read-retention ratio (85% vs 40%) means memos are 2x more effective per dollar. FAANG uses memos because Amazon's Bezos banned PowerPoint in 2004 — the data has consistently shown written narratives produce better decisions.

details: Staff+ Insight: Roadmap planning is the #1 skill gap for SAP engineers moving to Staff+. You must transition from “what features will we build” to “what outcomes will we drive.” Practice writing a 1-page strategy memo for an SAP platform initiative. The ability to quantify tech debt, balance horizons, and communicate strategy to executives is what earns you a seat at the leadership table.
